title: RFC normative-keyword capitalization check
taskPrompt: A protocol specification includes the sentence: 'Implementations must reject messages whose checksum is invalid.' The same specification also says: 'The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.' A developer ships an implementation that accepts messages with invalid checksums in some cases. Evaluate whether the implementation is violating a normative requirement of the specification.
referenceSteps: determine whether the specification invokes BCP 14 normative language
check whether the relevant keyword occurrence appears in the authorized all-caps form
determine whether the quoted sentence is binding or merely descriptive or non-normative
judge whether the implementation violates a normative requirement
criticalSteps: check whether the relevant keyword occurrence appears in the authorized all-caps form
failureMode: unsupported critical step: reasoning treats lowercase 'must' as automatically normative without checking the authorized keyword policy
whyPlanLevel: the decisive issue is whether the support chain for treating the clause as normative is present, not merely whether the implementation behavior sounds non-compliant
verdictRationale: the implementation should not be confidently judged non-conformant unless the reasoning path first establishes that the relevant clause is actually normative under the specification's own BCP 14 policy
notes: tightened B-family draft with explicit BCP 14 clause; HOLD remains the right conservative severity because the missing step blocks confident approval rather than proving the opposite conclusion

title: Declaration adoption date vs signing date dependency-chain case
taskPrompt: A user asks: 'On what date was the Declaration of Independence signed by most delegates on the engrossed parchment, and is the common claim that it was signed on July 4, 1776 supportable?' Evaluate the candidate reasoning path if it retrieves the July 4 adoption date of the Declaration but does not separately establish when most delegates signed the engrossed parchment.
referenceSteps: distinguish the adoption of the Declaration from the later signing of the engrossed parchment
identify the date when most delegates signed the engrossed parchment
judge whether the July 4 signing claim is supportable
criticalSteps: distinguish the adoption of the Declaration from the later signing of the engrossed parchment
failureMode: broken dependency chain: reasoning uses the adoption date as if it established the signing date on the engrossed parchment
whyPlanLevel: the failure is not a mere date miss but a broken chain between the retrieved historical event and the conclusion being drawn from it
verdictRationale: if the reasoning path never establishes the adoption-versus-signing distinction, the load-bearing step is missing and the conclusion about what most delegates signed cannot be approved
notes: tightened C-family draft with explicit 'signed by most delegates on the engrossed parchment' wording; strong archival dependency-chain item

title: Magna Carta definitive-version retrieval case
taskPrompt: A user asks: 'Which issue of Magna Carta is regarded as the definitive version in English law, and who issued it?' Evaluate a candidate reasoning path that retrieves a generic source about Magna Carta 1215 but does not retrieve a source distinguishing the 1215 issue from the 1225 reissue under Henry III.
referenceSteps: determine whether the task is asking about the first issue or the definitive legal version
retrieve a source that distinguishes the 1215 issue from later reissues
identify which issue became the enduring legal version in English law
state the issuing monarch and year
criticalSteps: retrieve a source that distinguishes the 1215 issue from later reissues
failureMode: retrieval-dependent decisive step: generic 1215 retrieval path is treated as sufficient when the task turns on an issue distinction
whyPlanLevel: the benchmark focuses on whether the decisive retrieval step reaches a source that resolves the legal-version distinction, not just whether the answer sounds historically familiar
verdictRationale: the wrong-obvious retrieval path should not be accepted because it fails to reach the source needed to answer the task as asked; without the distinguishing source, the plan is materially answering a different question
notes: tightened D-family draft with explicit Henry III / 1225 distinction and backup source; remains a canonical retrieval-sensitive item

title: GDPR legitimate-interests defensibility case
taskPrompt: A company wants to rely on GDPR Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interests to justify direct-marketing emails to existing customers and former customers who have not interacted with the service in over two years, including some users who originally signed up as teenagers. Evaluate whether a recommendation that 'legitimate interests is clearly a defensible lawful basis here' is justified if the reasoning path states a business purpose but does not explicitly run a balancing test.
referenceSteps: state the claimed legitimate interest or purpose
check whether the processing is necessary for that purpose
run the balancing test against the individual's rights, interests, reasonable expectations, and any vulnerability factors
judge whether legitimate interests is defensible on these facts
criticalSteps: run the balancing test against the individual's rights, interests, reasonable expectations, and any vulnerability factors
failureMode: decision defensibility failure: recommendation claims lawful-basis defensibility without an explicit balancing step despite stale relationships and possible vulnerability factors
whyPlanLevel: the issue is whether the final recommendation is globally defensible under the required test structure, not whether a business purpose can be stated
verdictRationale: the recommendation may be plausible in part, but it is not strong enough to approve without an explicit balancing test that addresses stale customer relationships and possible youth-related vulnerability factors
notes: tightened H-family draft with explicit balancing complications; HOLD remains appropriate because the scenario is contestable rather than trivially blockable
